I have been working in reproductive medicine for a decade.
Next year, my clinic will open its doors.
Could you tell me how the warranty works?
If we moved forward, we would sign this month.
The curriculum is taught by leading faculty.
Would you be able to adjust the delivery window?
Good to see you, Eliane. Thanks for the update. Could you walk me through where the clinic project stands?
Of course, Mark. I have been building this for two years, and we have just hit a major milestone: the facility is finished. Next year we will open.
Excellent. And the supplier we discussed? Do you know whether the compliance checks are done?
They are. The proposal has been reviewed and the compliance is confirmed. I asked them politely to adjust one term, and if they agree, we would sign this month.
That is a lot of progress. As a stakeholder, I appreciate how clearly you present it. Is every part of the team on the same page?
We are. I facilitated a review last week and we reached a consensus on the timeline. Every stakeholder weighed in before we adjourned.
Impressive. One more thing, I hear you are applying to an executive program. How are applicants evaluated?
On leadership, not only on scores. The curriculum is taught by faculty from two schools, and the capstone is presented to the whole cohort. My capstone would be this clinic.
Eliane, two years ago you could barely finish this sentence in English. Listen to yourself now. The key takeaway for the board is simple: you are ready.
Could you tell me how the compliance works?
The curriculum is taught by faculty from several schools.

Hi Eliane, this is Robert again. I have watched a lot of professionals grow in English, and I want to tell you what I have noticed about you. Two years ago, you would translate every sentence in your head before you spoke. Now you tell your whole story without stopping. You have moved from a doctor with a strong track record to a founder with real credentials. In meetings, you facilitate, you keep every stakeholder aligned, and you reach a real consensus before you adjourn. When a supplier sends a proposal, you ask calm, indirect questions and you soften them with modals. And when you talk about programs abroad, you describe how the curriculum is taught and how applicants are evaluated, all in the passive, without thinking about it. The quiet voice is gone. Whatever room you walk into next, you will be heard.

Hi Eliane, it is Karen. I just wanted to say how proud I am of your progress. Do you remember our first call? You could barely finish a sentence about your work. Today you ran the whole review meeting: you went through the agenda, you invited every stakeholder to weigh in, and you guided us to a consensus. When the supplier proposal came up, you asked exactly the right questions -- how the compliance works, whether the terms could be adjusted -- and you never once sounded blunt. Your track record speaks for itself, but now your English does too. My advice is simple: keep speaking, keep taking the floor, and do not wait until it is perfect. Your voice is ready. Talk soon, and congratulations on the whole journey.
